articles.headline: Formula 1 to Cancel Bahrain and Saudi Arabia Races Over Iran War
articles.subheadline: The sport faces a commercial hit exceeding £100 million as Middle East conflict makes hosting events too dangerous
articles.body:
Formula 1 is set to cancel next month's Bahrain and Saudi Arabia Grands Prix due to the ongoing war between the United States, Israel, and Iran, eliminating two of the calendar's highest-paying races and costing the sport more than £100 million in lost revenue.

While a formal announcement has not yet been issued, a decision is expected before the end of the weekend, according to the BBC's F1 correspondent Andrew Benson. The cancellation is driven by hard logistical deadlines — freight shipments to the Middle East would need to begin within days — and the judgment that staging major sporting events in the region would expose personnel to unacceptable risk. The Bahraini capital has already been struck by missiles, and the Saudi city of Jeddah is similarly deemed too dangerous.

The Bahrain Grand Prix had been scheduled for April 12, with the Saudi Arabian race in Jeddah set for the following weekend. Formula 1 explored replacement venues including Portimão in Portugal, Imola in Italy, and Istanbul Park in Turkey, but organizers concluded there was insufficient time to prepare any of those circuits and virtually no prospect of securing a hosting fee to offset the financial blow. Neither race will be replaced, trimming the 2024 season from 24 to 22 grands prix.

The financial consequences are significant. Bahrain and Saudi Arabia pay among the highest hosting fees on the F1 calendar, and the combined loss of more than £100 million will be felt across the sport. Those funds are split between the ten competing teams and Liberty Media's Formula One Group, the commercial rights holder, meaning each party stands to lose tens of millions of pounds depending on the prize money distribution formula.

The cancellations will create a five-week gap between the Japanese Grand Prix on March 29 and the Miami Grand Prix on May 3. After that hiatus, the season is expected to proceed as scheduled. The decision underscores how the widening Middle East conflict is reverberating far beyond the region, disrupting global sporting calendars and the commercial infrastructure that supports them.

articles.headline: Deep Learning Reveals 80,000 Earthquakes in Santorini Crisis
articles.subheadline: AI-enhanced seismic monitoring multiplied detected quakes 20-fold, uncovering fluid-driven volcanic processes in real time
articles.body:
An international team of 23 scientists used deep learning algorithms to transform earthquake detection during the 2025 Santorini seismic crisis, expanding the known catalogue from roughly 4,000 to 80,000 earthquakes and revealing a volcanic-tectonic event unprecedented in modern seismology.

The research, detailed in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server, describes how the team processed continuous seismic waveforms daily from February 1 through March 3, 2025, as intense seismic unrest unfolded between Santorini and Amorgos islands in the Aegean Sea. The AI-enhanced catalogue — representing a 20-fold increase in detected events — allowed researchers to identify distinctive burst-like, spasmodic seismicity swarms characteristic of fluid-driven processes from the earliest stages of the crisis. More than 200 earthquakes exceeding magnitude 4.0 struck within just a few weeks, concentrated in episodic bursts that the authors say have no precedent at any other volcanic system worldwide.

Led by Margarita Segou and Foteini Dervisi, with collaborators from institutions including Stanford University, the U.S. Geological Survey, and Italy's National Institute of Geophysics and Volcanology, the group performed detailed moment tensor inversions on early events. These analyses revealed significant non-double-couple components — a technical signature indicating that magmatic or high-pressure hydrothermal fluids, rather than simple fault slip, were driving the unrest. Concurrent deep-learning-enhanced tomography identified a previously unknown third magmatic reservoir beneath Anydros Islet, a small volcanic island between Santorini and Amorgos, consistent with pressure-driven volcanic processes at depth.

The findings underscore the growing role of artificial intelligence in volcanology and disaster response. Traditional seismic networks detected only a fraction of the actual earthquake activity, meaning that without the deep learning workflow, scientists and civil authorities would have had a far less complete picture of the crisis as it developed. The near-real-time processing capability proved critical, enabling the expert group to characterize the volcanic-tectonic nature of the swarm while events were still ongoing.

Santorini, famous for a catastrophic Bronze Age eruption that devastated the Minoan civilization, remains one of the most closely watched volcanic systems in Europe. The 2025 crisis drew global attention and prompted rapid mobilization of scientific resources. This study demonstrates that modern machine learning tools can dramatically sharpen the resolution of seismic monitoring during volcanic emergencies, potentially giving authorities earlier and more accurate warnings about evolving hazards.

articles.headline: Physicists Solve 20-Year Nuclear Mystery in the Cosmic Origin of Gold
articles.subheadline: University of Tennessee team achieves first measurement of beta-delayed two-neutron emission along the r-process pathway
articles.body:
Nuclear physicists have cracked open a two-decade-old mystery about how the universe forges gold, delivering three discoveries in a single study that reshape understanding of the extreme nuclear reactions inside collapsing and colliding stars.

A team led by researchers at the University of Tennessee at Knoxville, working at CERN's ISOLDE facility in Geneva, performed the first-ever measurement of neutron energies associated with beta-delayed two-neutron emission — a fleeting nuclear process that occurs along the rapid neutron-capture pathway, or r-process, responsible for creating heavy elements such as gold and platinum. The findings, published in Physical Review Letters with graduate student Peter Dyszel as first author, also include the first observation of a single-particle neutron state in tin-133 that theorists had predicted but no one had detected in 20 years of searching.

The r-process unfolds during some of the most violent events in the cosmos: supernovae, stellar collapses, and neutron star mergers. During these cataclysms, atomic nuclei absorb neutrons in rapid succession, becoming increasingly heavy and unstable before decaying into lighter, more stable forms. A critical step along this chain involves a parent nucleus undergoing beta decay and then releasing two neutrons — but the nuclei involved are so rare and short-lived that direct experimental study has been nearly impossible. "These nuclei are hard to make and require a lot of new technology to synthesize in sufficient quantities," said Robert Grzywacz, a UT professor and senior member of the collaboration. The team started with large quantities of indium-134, produced and purified using advanced laser separation techniques at CERN, then tracked its decay into excited forms of tin-134, tin-133, and tin-132 using a custom neutron detector built at UT with National Science Foundation funding.

The second discovery — the long-sought neutron state in tin-133 — overturned a prevailing assumption that decaying tin nuclei behave as "amnesiacs," losing all trace of how they were formed. Instead, the data showed that the nucleus retains a structural "memory" of the parent indium nucleus, an intermediate stage visible only through the two-neutron emission channel. "People were searching for it for 20 years and we found it," Grzywacz said. A third result compounded the significance: the newly identified state is populated in a non-statistical way, meaning the decay does not follow the probability patterns that standard models predict. This suggests that as physicists probe increasingly exotic nuclei far from stability — including superheavy elements like Tennessine — current theoretical frameworks may need fundamental revision.

The implications extend well beyond nuclear structure. Accurate models of the r-process are essential for interpreting observations from neutron star mergers, such as the gravitational-wave event GW170817 detected in 2017, which provided the first direct evidence that such collisions produce heavy elements. By supplying precise laboratory benchmarks for nuclear decay along the r-process pathway, the Tennessee team's work gives astrophysicists sharper tools for modeling element formation across the universe. The collaboration spanned multiple institutions and countries, reflecting the scale of infrastructure — from CERN's isotope factory to bespoke detector arrays — now required to interrogate the nuclear reactions that ultimately seed the cosmos with precious metals.

articles.headline: Google Closes $32B Wiz Acquisition in Largest VC-Backed Deal Ever
articles.subheadline: Index Ventures partner calls the cybersecurity mega-deal the 'Deal of the Decade,' citing convergence of AI, cloud, and security spending
articles.body:
Google has finalized its $32 billion acquisition of cybersecurity startup Wiz, completing what now stands as the largest venture-backed acquisition in history — a deal that one prominent investor is calling the "Deal of the Decade."

The transaction, which closed after clearing antitrust reviews on both sides of the Atlantic, represents a dramatic turnaround from 2024, when Wiz reportedly declined an earlier offer from the search giant. Google ultimately added an extra $9 billion to secure the deal, underscoring the strategic premium it placed on Wiz's cloud security platform. Shardul Shah, a partner at Index Ventures, told TechCrunch's Equity podcast that Wiz sits "at the center of three tailwinds: AI, cloud, and security spend" — a confluence of market forces that drove the company's extraordinary valuation.

The acquisition gives Google a major foothold in the rapidly expanding cloud security market at a time when enterprises are racing to protect increasingly complex AI-driven infrastructure. Wiz, founded in Israel in 2020, built a cloud-native security platform that quickly attracted major enterprise customers and became one of the fastest-growing startups in cybersecurity history. Its technology scans cloud environments for vulnerabilities without requiring agents, a capability that proved highly attractive as organizations migrated workloads to multi-cloud architectures.

The deal also arrives during a period of heightened M&A activity across the tech sector. In the same week, Meta completed its acquisition of Moltbook, described as a viral AI agent social network, signaling that large technology companies are aggressively pursuing deals to bolster their positions in AI-adjacent markets. Meanwhile, the broader tech industry continues to grapple with policy tensions, as employees at OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft signed a legal brief supporting Anthropic in an ongoing dispute with the Department of Defense — a reminder that the intersection of AI and government remains deeply contested.

For the venture capital ecosystem, the Wiz deal sets a new benchmark. It surpasses previous records for VC-backed exits and could encourage more aggressive investment in cybersecurity startups positioned at the intersection of cloud infrastructure and artificial intelligence. Whether the $32 billion price tag ultimately proves justified will depend on Google's ability to integrate Wiz's technology into its cloud platform and compete more effectively against rivals Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure in the enterprise security market.

articles.headline: Tech Giants Slide Into Correction as Mideast Conflict Drives Oil Past $100
articles.subheadline: Escalating war in Iran hammers major technology stocks as surging crude prices rattle broader markets
articles.body:
Major technology stocks fell into correction territory on Friday as the escalating conflict in Iran sent oil prices surging past $100 a barrel, dragging down equity markets and amplifying fears of a prolonged economic disruption.

The selloff marks a significant inflection point for the tech sector, which had been among the market's strongest performers before geopolitical turmoil overtook investor sentiment. A correction is typically defined as a decline of 10 percent or more from a recent peak. The downturn in tech megacaps — companies that had served as the backbone of the broader rally — signals a deepening risk-off mood across Wall Street as the Middle East crisis shows no signs of abating.

Crude oil closing above $100 per barrel underscores the economic stakes of the widening conflict. Elevated energy prices threaten to reignite inflationary pressures at a time when central banks have been navigating a delicate path toward monetary easing. For technology companies in particular, higher input costs and the prospect of reduced consumer and enterprise spending pose a dual headwind that has prompted investors to reassess valuations.

The broader stock market declined alongside the tech sector, reflecting concern that the war's economic fallout could extend well beyond energy markets. Defense and commodity-linked sectors have outperformed in recent sessions, but gains in those areas have done little to offset the weight of losses among the market's largest constituents. Analysts noted that the speed of the correction highlights how concentrated equity market gains had become in a handful of technology names, leaving indexes vulnerable to sharp reversals.

Investors will be closely watching developments in the Middle East over the coming days, along with any signals from the Federal Reserve on how the geopolitical landscape might affect the trajectory of interest rate policy. With oil prices elevated and uncertainty mounting, market participants face the prospect of sustained volatility heading into the second quarter of 2026.

articles.headline: Druckenmiller: Stablecoins Will Power Global Payments Within 15 Years
articles.subheadline: The billionaire investor praised blockchain-based tokens as faster and cheaper, while dismissing most of crypto as 'a solution looking for a problem'
articles.body:
Legendary investor Stanley Druckenmiller has predicted that stablecoins will become the backbone of global payment systems within the next 10 to 15 years, offering one of the most bullish endorsements yet of the fiat-pegged tokens from a figure in traditional finance — even as he renewed his broad skepticism of the cryptocurrency industry.

In an interview published Thursday by Morgan Stanley, Druckenmiller called stablecoins "efficient, quicker and cheaper" than existing payment infrastructure, singling out Tether's USDT and Circle's USDC as examples of blockchain technology delivering genuine productivity gains. "I assume our whole payment systems will be stablecoins in 10 or 15 years," the 72-year-old said. His assessment echoes a recent report from Australian investment bank Macquarie, which argued that stablecoins are rapidly evolving from niche crypto trading instruments into a foundational layer of global finance.

Yet Druckenmiller drew a sharp line between stablecoins and the rest of the digital asset universe. Repeating a critique he has voiced for years, he dismissed most cryptocurrency projects as "a solution looking for a problem." The comment underscores a growing bifurcation in institutional sentiment: warming enthusiasm for blockchain-based payments and tokenized assets alongside persistent doubts about the utility of thousands of alternative tokens.

On bitcoin specifically, Druckenmiller struck an ambivalent tone. He acknowledged the asset has cemented its status as a store of value — "it's become a brand, and people love it" — but expressed disappointment that its primary use case settled there rather than in payments or other applications. Bitcoin was trading near $71,400 at the time of publication.

Perhaps most provocatively, Druckenmiller questioned the long-term dominance of the U.S. dollar as the world's reserve currency, a position he has staked out since at least 2021. "I doubt it'll be the reserve currency in 50 years, but I don't have a clue what would be," he said, adding with characteristic bluntness: "Maybe some crypto thing I hate." The remarks arrive as Washington advances stablecoin legislation and regulators increasingly grapple with how to integrate digital assets into the existing financial architecture — a process Druckenmiller's comments suggest may ultimately be far more sweeping than current policy debates envision.

articles.headline: Circle Surpasses BlackRock as Top Tokenized Treasury Provider
articles.subheadline: Circle's USYC fund reaches $2.2 billion as the broader tokenized U.S. Treasury market hits a record $11 billion
articles.body:
Circle, the company behind the USDC stablecoin, has overtaken BlackRock to become the largest issuer of tokenized U.S. Treasury products, as the broader market for blockchain-based government debt surged to a record $11 billion. Circle's USYC token now commands roughly $2.2 billion in supply, edging past BlackRock's BUIDL fund — issued in partnership with tokenization firm Securitize — which holds approximately $2 billion in assets, according to data from RWA.xyz.

The shift marks a striking reversal of fortune in a market BlackRock once dominated. BUIDL's market share has fallen to 18% from a peak of 46% in May as new competitors flooded into the space. Circle acquired Hashnote, the original issuer of USYC, in early 2025, giving the stablecoin giant a foothold in the fast-growing tokenized funds sector. Much of USYC's recent expansion traces back to a single catalyst: its adoption on BNB Chain, where Binance introduced the token in July as off-exchange collateral for institutional derivatives trading. Since that launch, USYC supply on BNB Chain has ballooned to $1.84 billion — accounting for the vast majority of the fund's total assets.

"Tokenized treasuries and repo as collateral is a major emerging use case and we are proud of how quickly this has grown," Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire wrote on X on Friday. The arrangement allows institutional traders to earn yield on their Treasury-backed tokens while simultaneously deploying them as collateral, improving capital efficiency compared to holding idle stablecoins or cash.

The broader tokenized Treasury market has added roughly $2.5 billion in value since January, a 27% increase year to date. Notably, much of that growth coincided with a downturn in cryptocurrency prices, suggesting that traders are rotating into yield-bearing tokenized instruments as a way to park capital while awaiting better entry points in digital asset markets. The appeal is straightforward: blockchain-based Treasury tokens offer near-instant settlement, transparent reserves, and round-the-clock accessibility — features that traditional money-market infrastructure cannot match.

The competitive landscape is intensifying. BlackRock, which pioneered institutional credibility in the tokenized asset space, now faces pressure not only from Circle but from a growing roster of entrants targeting the intersection of traditional fixed income and decentralized finance. Securitize, BUIDL's tokenization partner, did not respond to requests for comment. For Circle, the milestone underscores a broader strategic pivot beyond stablecoins into the infrastructure layer of onchain finance — a bet that tokenized real-world assets will become a cornerstone of how capital moves in the years ahead.

articles.headline: U.S.-Iran Conflict Hits Two-Week Mark as Voters Question Strategy
articles.subheadline: Pentagon reports 15,000 targets struck as Iran threatens to widen war and keep Strait of Hormuz closed
articles.body:
The United States military has escalated its air campaign against Iran to what officials describe as unprecedented levels, with the U.S.-Israeli alliance striking approximately 15,000 targets since hostilities began two weeks ago. Yet as the conflict intensifies, American voters remain uncertain about the administration's strategic objectives, according to polling experts.

The Pentagon has deployed a Marine expeditionary unit to the Middle East as the campaign broadens, signaling a deepening commitment to sustained military operations. On the opposing side, Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued stark warnings that Tehran intends to keep the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply transits — effectively closed, and threatened to open additional fronts if U.S. and Israeli strikes continue. The threat to one of the world's most critical energy chokepoints carries significant implications for global oil markets and supply chains already under strain.

Despite the scale of the military operation, public opinion polling suggests the Trump administration has not yet made a convincing case to the American electorate about its endgame. Cliff Young, chair of Ipsos U.S. Public Affairs and a professor at Texas A&M University's Bush School of Government and Public Service, highlighted the disconnect between the administration's military escalation and public understanding of the conflict's goals. The ambiguity echoes historical patterns in which initial public tolerance for military action erodes quickly absent a clearly articulated mission and timeline.

The conflict's rapid escalation — from initial strikes to a full-scale air campaign encompassing thousands of targets in just fourteen days — has outpaced the political groundwork typically laid for sustained overseas military engagement. With Iran threatening asymmetric retaliation and the potential for disruption to global energy supplies growing, the stakes extend well beyond the immediate theater of war. Markets have already priced in elevated geopolitical risk, and any prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz could trigger an energy crisis with worldwide economic consequences.

As the campaign enters its third week, the administration faces mounting pressure to articulate a clear strategic framework — both to reassure allies and to maintain domestic support for what is shaping up to be the most significant U.S. military engagement in the Middle East in over two decades.

articles.headline: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Framework Targets Protein pKa Prediction
articles.subheadline: Researchers combine quantum-inspired feature mapping with deep neural networks to improve protein electrostatics modeling
articles.body:
A new computational framework blending quantum-inspired mathematics with classical biochemistry aims to solve one of the persistent challenges in protein science: accurately predicting the acidity constants of individual amino acid residues. The approach, detailed in a preprint posted to arXiv on March 9 by researchers Van Le and Tan Le, introduces what the authors call a Deep Quantum Neural Network (DQNN) that outperforms purely classical models in generalizing across diverse biochemical contexts.

Predicting residue-level pKa values — a measure of how readily specific amino acids in a protein donate or accept protons — is critical for understanding protein folding, enzymatic activity, drug binding, and molecular stability. Existing computational methods rely on classical descriptors drawn from databases such as DeepKaDB and datasets generated by constant-pH molecular dynamics simulations. However, these approaches often falter when applied to protein environments that differ significantly from their training data.

The new framework addresses this limitation by augmenting traditional structural descriptors with a Gaussian kernel-based quantum-inspired feature mapping. This mathematical transformation projects residue-level information into a higher-dimensional space, enabling the neural network to capture nonlinear relationships in the microenvironments surrounding individual residues — relationships that classical encodings alone struggle to represent. The quantum-enhanced descriptors are then merged with normalized structural features to create a unified hybrid input for the DQNN architecture.

Benchmarking results reported in the paper indicate that the DQNN achieves stronger cross-context generalization compared to classical baselines across multiple curated descriptor sets. External validation on the PKAD-R experimental benchmark and a case study involving the amyloid-beta 40 peptide — a molecule central to Alzheimer's disease research — further demonstrated the model's robustness and transferability to real experimental data.

Notably, the framework is described as "quantum-inspired" rather than requiring actual quantum hardware, making it immediately deployable on classical computing infrastructure. The authors frame their contribution as a scalable approach applicable not only to pKa prediction but to broader problems in protein electrostatics, potentially accelerating drug design and enzyme engineering workflows. The work sits at the intersection of chemical physics, artificial intelligence, and neural computing, reflecting a growing trend of borrowing mathematical formalisms from quantum mechanics to enhance machine learning architectures even in the absence of quantum processors.

articles.headline: Global Stocks Slide as Brent Crude Surges Past $100 Amid Iran War
articles.subheadline: Oil hits three-year high as escalating conflict stokes inflation fears, sending bonds and equities lower
articles.body:
Global equity markets sold off sharply on Wednesday as the ongoing war in Iran pushed Brent crude above $100 a barrel for the first time in more than three years, reigniting fears that surging energy costs will feed a new wave of inflation across major economies.

The spike in oil prices reverberated across asset classes on March 12, hammering stocks on Wall Street and overwhelming an earlier rally in government bonds. Investors who had initially sought safety in Treasuries reversed course as the implications of persistently elevated crude became clear: higher energy costs threaten to keep inflation sticky, potentially constraining central banks from easing monetary policy even as economic growth comes under pressure from geopolitical uncertainty.

The conflict in Iran, which has intensified in recent weeks, sits at the heart of the market turmoil. Iran is a major oil-producing nation and a critical player in Middle Eastern energy infrastructure. Disruptions to its output — or the mere threat of sustained supply constraints — have historically been enough to jolt global commodity markets. With Brent now trading above the psychologically significant $100 threshold, analysts warn that consumer energy prices in the United States and Europe could climb further in the weeks ahead, squeezing household budgets and corporate margins alike.

Volatility indicators surged as traders scrambled to reprice risk. The combination of falling equities and rising commodity prices presents a particularly difficult environment for portfolio managers, many of whom had positioned for a benign inflation trajectory heading into the second quarter of 2026. The bond market's inability to sustain its initial flight-to-safety bid underscored the tension between recession hedging and inflation protection — a dynamic that has not dominated fixed-income trading to this degree since the energy shocks of 2022.

Market participants will be closely watching diplomatic developments around the Iran conflict as well as upcoming economic data releases for signals on whether central banks might adjust their policy stances. For now, the message from markets is unambiguous: geopolitical risk has reasserted itself as the dominant force driving global asset prices, and investors are bracing for further turbulence.

articles.headline: Oil Prices Surge 40% as Strait of Hormuz Blockage Shows No End
articles.subheadline: Traders brace for prolonged supply disruption as the Iran conflict creates the worst oil market crisis in history
articles.body:
Global oil markets are facing their most severe supply disruption on record as the ongoing conflict with Iran continues to choke flows through the Strait of Hormuz, with crude prices now up 40% since hostilities began and traders increasingly pessimistic about a near-term resolution.

The strategic waterway, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption typically passes, remains effectively blocked as the conflict drags on. Market participants had initially hoped the disruption would prove short-lived, but sentiment has shifted sharply as diplomatic efforts have failed to produce a ceasefire or any mechanism to restore safe passage for tankers. The blockage now represents the single largest sustained supply interruption the modern oil market has experienced, surpassing previous crises including the 1973 Arab oil embargo and the 1990 Iraqi invasion of Kuwait in terms of barrels removed from the market.

The 40% price surge is rippling through the global economy, raising costs for manufacturers, airlines, and consumers alike. Gasoline prices at the pump have climbed steeply across the United States, Europe, and Asia, stoking inflation fears just as central banks had been navigating a delicate path toward rate normalization. Economists warn that if the disruption persists for weeks or months longer, the energy shock could tip vulnerable economies into recession and force policymakers to choose between fighting inflation and supporting growth.

Strategic petroleum reserves in the United States, Europe, and Asia have provided some buffer, with coordinated releases helping to partially offset lost supply. OPEC+ members with spare capacity, particularly Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, have pledged to ramp up production, but logistics and infrastructure constraints limit how quickly additional barrels can reach the market — especially with alternative shipping routes around the Cape of Good Hope adding weeks to delivery times and straining global tanker availability.

Traders and analysts are now pricing in the possibility that the Hormuz disruption could last well into the second quarter, a scenario that would test the resilience of global supply chains and energy security frameworks built over decades. Options markets reflect growing demand for upside protection, with call spreads on Brent crude suggesting some participants are hedging against prices climbing significantly higher from current levels.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Retreats 3.5% From Near $74,000 as Iran Tensions Rattle Markets
articles.subheadline: U.S. military deployments to the Middle East abruptly reversed a broad crypto rally, while oil surged nearly $5 per barrel
articles.body:
Bitcoin shed 3.5% in a matter of hours on Friday after news of escalating U.S. military activity in the Middle East abruptly halted what had been a promising rally across risk assets. The leading cryptocurrency surged to nearly $74,000 earlier in the session — its highest level in roughly a month — before reversing to just above $71,200 as geopolitical headlines hit the wires.

The catalyst was a pair of developments that signaled deepening U.S. involvement in the Iran conflict. The U.S. Central Command confirmed that all six crew members aboard a refueling aircraft that crashed in Iraq on Thursday were killed, while the Wall Street Journal reported the Pentagon is deploying a Marine expeditionary unit of approximately 2,500 troops to the region, including forces attached to the USS Tripoli. Iran has reportedly stepped up attacks near the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of global oil supply transits.

The shock waves were not confined to crypto. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq, both positive in early trading, flipped to losses of 0.4% to 0.5%. Oil prices jumped more than $5 per barrel from session lows, with crude trading near $97.30 — up nearly 2% on the day. Gold, traditionally a haven during geopolitical crises, counterintuitively extended a recent pullback by another 1%, suggesting traders may be raising cash across the board rather than rotating into safe havens.

Other major digital assets followed bitcoin's trajectory. Ethereum, Solana, and Dogecoin each pulled back from session highs, though all remained roughly 3% higher over the prior 24 hours. Crypto-linked equities showed more resilience: Marathon Digital rose 10%, while Galaxy Digital, Bitmine, and Cipher Mining posted gains of 5% to 7%.

Paul Howard, director at trading firm Wincent, characterized the selloff as likely transitory. "These headlines tend to have a short half-life," he said, adding that he would expect the impact to be "short-lived" absent concrete follow-up military action. Howard noted that broader optimism — including expectations around Russian sanctions relief — had been underpinning the rally before the Iran headlines intervened. Whether that momentum can reassert itself may depend on how rapidly the situation in the Strait of Hormuz evolves over the weekend.

articles.headline: First Photo of Prince Andrew, Mandelson, and Epstein Together Surfaces in DOJ Files
articles.subheadline: The image, believed taken around 1999-2000 at Martha's Vineyard, emerged from millions of pages released by the US Department of Justice
articles.body:
A photograph showing Prince Andrew, former Labour minister Peter Mandelson, and convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein seated together has been discovered among the vast tranche of documents released by the U.S. Department of Justice — the first known image placing all three men in the same frame.

The photo, which shows the trio around a wooden deck table adorned with American flag mugs, is believed to have been taken at Martha's Vineyard between 1999 and 2000, according to ITV News. That timeframe predates Epstein's 2008 guilty plea to state prostitution charges in Florida, which resulted in a 13-month prison sentence and his registration as a sex offender. The image was part of a massive document release in January comprising roughly three million pages, 180,000 images, and 2,000 videos that journalists and the public are still combing through.

The discovery adds another layer to the deepening legal troubles facing both British figures. Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, as he is now formally known after being stripped of his royal titles, was arrested in mid-February in Norfolk on suspicion of misconduct in public office. Thames Valley Police has been assessing a complaint that the former prince shared confidential material with Epstein during his decade-long tenure as a UK trade envoy from 2001 to 2011. He has repeatedly and strenuously denied any wrongdoing. Separately, Lord Mandelson was also arrested on suspicion of misconduct in public office and released under investigation. The Metropolitan Police launched a probe last month into allegations that Mandelson, while serving as a government minister, passed market-sensitive government information to Epstein. Mandelson was dismissed from his post as UK ambassador to the United States after emails surfaced in which he expressed support for Epstein in 2008, when the financier was facing sex offense charges.

The newly discovered photograph appears to match the location of another image that accompanied Mandelson's message in a "birthday book" assembled for Epstein's 50th birthday in 2003. Emails reviewed by the BBC show a 2013 exchange between Mandelson and Epstein's personal assistant, Lesley Groff, in which Groff suggested a photo may have been taken on "Jeffrey's island." Mandelson reportedly replied that he believed it was taken at Martha's Vineyard during his first meeting with Epstein. Both men's positions maintain they have not acted criminally; Mandelson has long said he accepted Epstein's account of his legal troubles and only learned the full truth after Epstein's death by suicide in a Manhattan jail in August 2019 while awaiting trial on federal sex trafficking charges.

The continuing examination of the DOJ files has already yielded other damaging material, including images appearing to show the former prince in compromising positions and photographs of Mandelson in his underwear. Epstein's former girlfriend, Ghislaine Maxwell, is currently serving a 20-year federal sentence for recruiting and trafficking teenage girls for sexual abuse. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer has acknowledged that appointing Mandelson as ambassador was a mistake, while Canada's prime minister has called for Andrew's removal from the line of succession. Neither man has been charged with a crime.

articles.headline: All Six Crew Members Killed in US KC-135 Tanker Crash in Iraq
articles.subheadline: The crash raises the US military death toll in the conflict with Iran to 13, as the Pentagon investigates the cause
articles.body:
All six crew members aboard a US Air Force KC-135 Stratotanker aerial refuelling aircraft were killed when the plane crashed in western Iraq on Thursday, US Central Command confirmed. The incident marks the deadliest single loss for American forces since the US-Israel war with Iran began two weeks ago, bringing the total US military death toll in the conflict to 13.

Centcom said the crash occurred at approximately 2:00 p.m. ET on Thursday and stressed that neither hostile nor enemy fire were involved. A second aircraft involved in the same mission landed safely. However, Iran's military claimed on state television that an allied group had struck the plane with a missile — an assertion at odds with the Pentagon's account. The crash site lies in a region of western Iraq where pro-Iranian militias are known to operate, adding complexity to the investigation now underway.

Gen. Dan Caine, chair of the US Joint Chiefs of Staff, confirmed Friday that the crew had been on a combat mission at the time of the crash. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called the fallen crew members "American heroes," saying their "sacrifice will only recommit us to the resolve of this mission." The identities of the six personnel were being withheld for 24 hours to allow notification of next of kin. A search and rescue operation had been launched after only four of the six deceased crew were initially located.

The KC-135 Stratotanker, manufactured by Boeing in the 1950s and early 1960s, has long served as the backbone of the US military's aerial refuelling fleet, enabling fighter jets and bombers to extend their range without landing. The aircraft typically carries a crew consisting of at least a pilot, co-pilot, and a boom operator who controls the refuelling arm. The loss is the fourth US military aircraft downed during the current conflict, following three F-15s lost earlier this month in what officials described as an apparent friendly fire incident over Kuwait, though all six crew members in that case ejected safely.

The crash comes amid an intensifying broader conflict. Israel launched what it described as a "wide-scale wave of strikes" targeting Iranian infrastructure across Tehran on Friday, while oil prices climbed above $100 per barrel after Iran's new supreme leader vowed to continue blocking the Strait of Hormuz. A French soldier was also killed by a drone strike at a Kurdish military base in northern Iraq on Thursday, underscoring the widening toll of the conflict across the region. President Donald Trump has suggested the war could end "very soon" but has also indicated the US could "go further."

articles.headline: U.S. Escalates Iran Strikes as Two-Week War Disrupts Global Oil Markets
articles.subheadline: Trump warns of 20-year rebuilding timeline as both sides signal further escalation near Strait of Hormuz
articles.body:
The United States has intensified its military campaign against Iran to what officials describe as unprecedented levels, as the conflict enters its third week with no signs of de-escalation and mounting consequences for global energy markets and financial stability.

President Donald Trump struck a defiant tone in a Fox News Radio interview aired Friday, warning that Iran would face devastating consequences regardless of its retaliatory posture. "They're going to be hit. They can talk all they want but, you know, let's see what they do," Trump said. "They are being hit so hard, it'll take them 20 years to rebuild." The president also acknowledged the possibility that Iran could target the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply passes — signaling awareness that the conflict's economic fallout could deepen considerably.

Oil prices have climbed as the war disrupts energy flows from one of the world's most critical petroleum-producing regions. The Strait of Hormuz, which connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman, has long been considered a strategic chokepoint whose closure or partial blockade could send crude prices surging and trigger economic shockwaves across importing nations in Asia and Europe. Both sides have now openly threatened further escalation, raising the specter of broader disruption to global supply chains already under strain.

The conflict, which began in late February, has also spilled into Lebanon, where airstrikes on March 13 targeted buildings in Abbasiyyeh, sending plumes of smoke over the area. The expansion of military operations beyond Iran's borders suggests a widening theater of engagement that could draw in additional regional actors and complicate diplomatic efforts to negotiate a ceasefire.

Financial markets have been rattled by the sustained hostilities. Commodity traders are closely monitoring the situation around the Strait of Hormuz for any indication that shipping lanes could be threatened, while equity markets have shown increased volatility amid the geopolitical uncertainty. With neither Washington nor Tehran signaling a willingness to pursue negotiations, analysts warn that the economic consequences — particularly for energy-dependent economies — could intensify sharply in the weeks ahead.

articles.headline: U.S. Treasury Sanctions Network That Laundered $800M in Crypto for North Korea
articles.subheadline: Six individuals and two companies allegedly converted funds across Ethereum, Tron, and Bitcoin to finance Pyongyang's weapons programs
articles.body:
The U.S. Treasury Department on Thursday sanctioned six individuals and two companies accused of helping North Korea convert approximately $800 million into cryptocurrency during 2024, laundering the proceeds to fund the country's weapons of mass destruction and ballistic missile programs.

The Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) identified a sprawling operation that placed North Korean IT workers into legitimate companies — including American firms — using forged documents, stolen identities, and fabricated personas. Once embedded, these operatives funneled the bulk of their wages back to Pyongyang and, in some cases, deployed malware to extract proprietary data and extort their employers. The network operated across multiple countries, including Vietnam, Laos, and Spain, according to the Treasury.

"The North Korean regime targets American companies through deceptive schemes carried out by its overseas IT operatives, who weaponize sensitive data and extort businesses for substantial payments," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement. Among those designated was Nguyen Quang Viet, CEO of Vietnam-based Quangvietdnbg International Services Co., who allegedly converted roughly $2.5 million into cryptocurrency for North Korean actors between mid-2023 and mid-2025.

OFAC's action flagged 21 crypto wallet addresses spanning Ethereum, Tron, and Bitcoin — a reflection of what blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis described as North Korea's increasingly "multichain" strategy for moving and obscuring illicit funds. The sanctioned network leveraged centralized exchanges, hosted wallets, decentralized finance protocols, and cross-chain bridges to route money beyond the reach of traditional enforcement. Chainalysis noted separately that DPRK-linked hackers stole a record $2 billion in cryptocurrency last year alone, underscoring the regime's deepening reliance on digital assets as a revenue source.

The sanctions freeze any U.S.-based assets held by the designated individuals and entities and prohibit American persons from transacting with them. The action highlights a growing tension at the intersection of crypto adoption and national security, as regulators work to close the gaps that state-sponsored actors exploit across decentralized financial infrastructure. For the broader digital asset industry, the case serves as a stark reminder that compliance frameworks remain a critical — and scrutinized — frontier.

articles.headline: Brent Crude Surges Past $100 as U.S.-Iran Conflict Threatens Strait of Hormuz
articles.subheadline: Oil markets face extreme volatility as Iran vows to keep critical shipping chokepoint closed amid escalating U.S. strikes
articles.body:
Brent crude oil breached the $100-per-barrel threshold on March 12, capping one of the most turbulent stretches in the history of global energy markets, as an escalating military confrontation between the United States and Iran sent shockwaves through commodity trading desks worldwide.

The surge was driven by Iran's pledge to maintain an effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption passes. The strait, situated between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula, serves as the sole maritime route for crude exports from major Gulf producers including Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Iraq. Its disruption — long considered a worst-case scenario for energy markets — now appears to be unfolding in real time as U.S. military forces step up strikes on Iranian targets.

Investors are bracing for further upheaval. The move above $100 marks a psychologically significant milestone that had not been tested since the aftermath of Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, when Brent briefly spiked above $130. Traders and analysts warn that sustained closure of the Hormuz chokepoint could push prices considerably higher, with some forecasts now modeling scenarios well above $120 if the conflict persists. The volatility has also rippled into broader financial markets, with equities wobbling and safe-haven assets rallying as geopolitical risk premiums are repriced across asset classes.

The immediate economic implications are stark. Higher energy costs threaten to reignite inflationary pressures that central banks in the U.S. and Europe have spent years trying to contain. Consumers could see the impact at fuel pumps within days, while energy-intensive industries face a sudden spike in input costs. Strategic petroleum reserves held by the United States and International Energy Agency member nations offer a potential buffer, though any coordinated release has yet to be announced.

For now, the trajectory of oil prices hinges on whether diplomatic channels can de-escalate the confrontation before the Strait of Hormuz disruption inflicts lasting damage on global supply chains. With both Washington and Tehran showing little sign of backing down, markets appear poised for continued turbulence in the days ahead.

articles.headline: Apple Quietly Cuts App Store Commission in China to 25%
articles.subheadline: The move, made after talks with Chinese regulators, contrasts sharply with Apple's prolonged battles in the EU and U.S.
articles.body:
Apple has agreed to reduce its App Store commission rate in China from 30% to 25%, a concession reached through discussions with Chinese regulators that stands in stark contrast to the company's protracted legal and regulatory fights in Western markets. The new rates take effect March 15, 2026.

The revised terms apply to paid apps and in-app purchases across the Chinese App Store. Auto-renewable subscriptions will also see a cut, dropping from 15% to 12% after their first year. Notably, Apple said the changes will not require developers to accept new licensing terms — a signal that the company wanted to implement the adjustment with minimal friction.

The speed and quietness of the move underscores China's outsized importance to Apple's bottom line. In its most recent fiscal first quarter, the company reported a 16% year-over-year surge in iPhone revenue from China, contributing to what Apple called a record-breaking quarter. Maintaining goodwill with Beijing's regulators is evidently a strategic priority the company is willing to pay for — literally.

The contrast with other jurisdictions is striking. In the European Union, Apple has spent years in a grinding back-and-forth with regulators over App Store commissions and sideloading requirements under the Digital Markets Act. In the United States, the company successfully defended itself against Epic Games' antitrust lawsuit, with a judge ruling Apple is not a monopoly, though developers did win the right to direct users to alternative payment methods. Apple has kept its standard 30% commission intact in the U.S., offering reduced rates only through targeted programs like its Small Business Program.

"We are committed to terms that remain fair and transparent to all developers, and to always offering competitive App Store rates to developers distributing apps in China that are no higher than overall rates in other markets," Apple said in its announcement, which was documented in an updated version of its Developer Program License Agreement. The carefully worded statement leaves the door open for further rate adjustments in other regions — or, just as plausibly, for Apple to argue that its China rates now set the ceiling rather than the floor for global commissions.

articles.headline: Blood Test Detects Alzheimer's by Reading Protein Shape, Not Quantity
articles.subheadline: Scripps Research team identifies structural changes in three plasma proteins that track disease progression with up to 93% accuracy
articles.body:
A novel blood test that analyzes the three-dimensional folding of proteins rather than their concentration has shown striking accuracy in distinguishing Alzheimer's patients from healthy individuals, according to research published February 27 in Nature Aging.

The study, led by Scripps Research Institute professor John Yates and senior scientist Casimir Bamberger, examined plasma samples from 520 participants divided into three groups: cognitively normal adults, individuals with mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and patients diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease. Using mass spectrometry and machine learning, the team identified structural alterations in three blood proteins — C1QA, an immune signaling protein; clusterin, which helps fold proteins and clear amyloid; and apolipoprotein B, a fat-transport molecule involved in vascular health — that closely tracked disease status. When classifying participants across all three categories, the method achieved roughly 83% overall accuracy. In head-to-head comparisons between two groups, such as healthy individuals versus those with MCI, accuracy exceeded 93%.

The approach represents a conceptual departure from conventional Alzheimer's blood diagnostics, which typically measure levels of amyloid beta and phosphorylated tau. While those biomarkers remain widely used, the Scripps team argues that protein misfolding — a hallmark of neurodegenerative disease — may produce detectable structural signatures in the bloodstream before traditional markers become informative. "The correlation was amazing," Bamberger said. "It was very surprising to find three lysine sites on three different proteins that correlate so highly with disease state."

Critically, the three-protein panel held up in independent validation groups and in repeated blood draws taken months apart, where it identified disease status with approximately 86% accuracy and reflected changes in diagnosis over time. The structural score also correlated strongly with cognitive test results and moderately with MRI measurements of brain atrophy, suggesting it captures biologically meaningful disease progression rather than statistical noise.

With an estimated 7.2 million Americans age 65 and older currently living with Alzheimer's, earlier and less invasive detection methods could significantly alter treatment timelines. "If treatment can start before significant damage has been done, it may be possible to better preserve long-term memory," Yates said. The team, which included collaborators at the University of Kansas Medical Center and the University of California San Diego, is now exploring whether the same structural profiling technique could be applied to Parkinson's disease and cancer. Larger clinical studies with longer follow-up periods will be needed before the test can move into routine medical practice.

articles.headline: All Six Crew Members Killed in US Military Tanker Crash Over Iraq
articles.subheadline: KC-135 refueling aircraft went down in friendly Iraqi airspace, US Central Command confirms
articles.body:
All six crew members aboard a US Air Force KC-135 Stratotanker refueling aircraft were killed when the plane crashed over western Iraq, US Central Command confirmed on Wednesday.

The KC-135, a aerial refueling platform that serves as a critical logistical backbone for US military flight operations across the Middle East, went down in what Central Command described as friendly Iraqi airspace. No further details on the cause of the crash were immediately released, and it remains unclear whether hostile action, mechanical failure, or other factors contributed to the incident.

The loss marks one of the deadliest single incidents involving US military personnel in the region in recent months. The KC-135 Stratotanker, a Boeing-built aircraft derived from the 707 airliner platform, has been in service with the US Air Force since the late 1950s and remains a workhorse of American aerial refueling capabilities. The aging fleet has long been slated for gradual replacement, with the newer KC-46 Pegasus tanker slowly entering operational service.

US Central Command, which oversees military operations across the Middle East and Central Asia, did not identify the crew members pending notification of next of kin. An investigation into the cause of the crash is expected to follow standard military protocols. The United States maintains a significant air presence in Iraq and the broader region as part of ongoing counterterrorism operations and strategic deterrence missions.

The crash is likely to draw renewed attention to the readiness and condition of the US military's aging tanker fleet, which is essential for projecting air power across vast distances in the Middle East theater. Defense officials have previously flagged concerns about the maintenance demands of the decades-old KC-135 airframes, even as the transition to next-generation refueling aircraft has faced delays and cost overruns.

articles.headline: FBI Probes Malware-Laced Games Distributed Through Steam Store
articles.subheadline: At least seven games published over two years allegedly served as Trojan horses to infect unsuspecting PC gamers
articles.body:
The Federal Bureau of Investigation has launched a probe into a suspected cybercriminal who published multiple malware-infected video games on Valve's Steam platform, the world's largest digital storefront for PC gaming. The agency issued a public call on Friday for potential victims who may have unknowingly installed the compromised titles.

The FBI identified seven games believed to have been developed by the same threat actor over the past two years: BlockBlasters, Chemia, Dashverse/DashFPS, Lampy, Lunara, PirateFi, and Tokenova. Each title was functional enough to pass as a legitimate, if rudimentary, game — but their true purpose was to serve as a delivery mechanism for malware, effectively operating as Trojan horses that exploited user trust in the Steam marketplace.

The incident underscores a persistent vulnerability in digital distribution platforms that host millions of titles from independent developers. Steam, which Valve operates, has faced similar episodes before. In 2025, hackers successfully uploaded games containing malicious code to the platform. Those titles were eventually removed, but not before an undisclosed number of users had already downloaded and installed them. The recurrence suggests that existing vetting processes may be insufficient to catch sophisticated attempts to weaponize game distribution channels.

Neither Valve nor the FBI responded to requests for comment regarding the scope of the investigation or the number of users potentially affected. The FBI's public solicitation of victims indicates the agency is still working to determine the full extent of the campaign, including what type of malware was deployed. Previous Steam-based attacks have involved infostealers — programs designed to harvest passwords, financial credentials, and other sensitive data from compromised machines.

The case highlights a growing challenge for platform operators that balance open access for independent developers against security risks. Steam hosts tens of thousands of games from small studios and solo creators, making comprehensive pre-publication security screening a significant logistical and technical undertaking. For gamers who may have installed any of the seven flagged titles, the FBI's announcement serves as an urgent prompt to scan their systems and monitor accounts for signs of compromise.

articles.headline: Motional Robotaxis Launch on Uber in Las Vegas After $1B Rescue
articles.subheadline: Hyundai's autonomous vehicle unit returns to commercial service two years after gutting 40% of its workforce and pivoting to AI
articles.body:
Motional, the Hyundai-owned autonomous vehicle company that nearly collapsed two years ago, has begun offering robotaxi rides through the Uber app in Las Vegas — marking a significant comeback for a startup that was forced to lay off nearly half its staff and completely rethink its technology approach.

Starting Friday, riders in Las Vegas can hail Motional's self-driving Hyundai Ioniq 5 vehicles for trips to and from five designated zones, including the Resorts World and Encore casinos on the Strip, the Westgate near the Las Vegas Convention Center, the Town Square shopping center near the airport, and curbside locations in Downtown Las Vegas. A human safety monitor will remain in each vehicle for now, though both companies said they expect to offer fully driverless service by the end of 2026. Riders cannot specifically request a robotaxi but can increase their chances of being matched with one by enabling autonomous vehicle pickup in the Uber app.

The launch caps a turbulent period for Motional, which was originally formed as a joint venture between Hyundai and Aptiv. By early 2024, the company had fallen behind on its goal of launching a robotaxi service with Lyft, and Aptiv withdrew its funding. Hyundai was left with a stark choice: walk away or double down. The South Korean automaker chose the latter, injecting an additional $1 billion into Motional while the company underwent a painful restructuring that eliminated roughly 40 percent of its workforce. The surviving team pivoted from the company's legacy software stack to a neural network-based approach to autonomous driving, a shift CEO Laura Major described at CES 2026 as a deliberate decision to "slow down in the near term so that we could speed up."

For Uber, the Motional integration is the latest addition to an increasingly crowded autonomous vehicle strategy. The ride-hail giant has signed partnerships with more than 25 AV companies worldwide over the past two years. In the same week as the Motional announcement, Uber revealed plans to deploy Wayve-powered self-driving Nissan Leafs in Tokyo and to bring Amazon-owned Zoox robotaxis onto its Las Vegas platform later this year. The approach positions Uber as a platform aggregator rather than a developer of its own self-driving technology — a strategy that contrasts sharply with rivals like Waymo, which operates its own fleet.

Motional has said it plans to expand beyond Las Vegas, with intentions to launch in multiple cities through both Uber and Lyft. But for now, the Vegas deployment serves as a critical proof point — both for Motional's retooled AI-first technology and for Hyundai's billion-dollar bet that autonomous driving remains a viable commercial opportunity despite the sector's long history of missed deadlines and broken promises.

articles.headline: European Stocks Rally as Hormuz Diplomatic Talks Ease Oil Fears
articles.subheadline: Oil dips below $100 a barrel after reports that European nations are negotiating safe passage through the critical strait
articles.body:
European equity markets staged a rebound on Friday after reports emerged that several European nations have opened negotiations with Iran to secure safe passage for commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, the vital shipping chokepoint that has been largely closed since the onset of the Iran conflict.

The diplomatic developments helped push oil prices back below $100 a barrel, providing relief to markets that have been battered by weeks of elevated energy costs and supply disruption fears. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption passes, has been effectively shut to commercial traffic amid the ongoing war, sending shockwaves through global energy markets and weighing heavily on European equities.

Friday's gains came as European stocks had been tracking toward a second consecutive weekly decline, reflecting the persistent drag of the Iran conflict on investor sentiment. The prospect of even a partial reopening of the strait to European-flagged tankers was enough to spark a broad-based rally, with energy-intensive sectors and transport stocks among the biggest beneficiaries. The easing in crude prices offered particular respite to European economies, which are heavily dependent on imported energy and have faced mounting inflationary pressures since the crisis escalated.

Analysts cautioned that any agreement on safe passage would likely be narrow in scope and fragile in practice, given the volatile military situation in the region. The talks reportedly involve multiple European governments seeking bilateral assurances from Tehran, though details of the negotiations and which nations are leading the effort remain unclear. The durability of the oil price retreat will depend heavily on whether these discussions produce concrete commitments or remain at the exploratory stage.

The broader market outlook remains clouded by uncertainty over the conflict's trajectory and its cascading effects on global trade. European indices have shed significant ground since hostilities began, and while Friday's rally offered a welcome reprieve, investors remain wary that any breakdown in diplomacy or escalation in military operations could rapidly reverse the gains. For now, the possibility of restoring even limited tanker traffic through the world's most important oil transit route has given markets a reason to cautiously rebuild positions.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Surges to $73,800 as Oil Fears Ease and Short Squeeze Builds
articles.subheadline: The cryptocurrency has gained 11% since the Iran conflict began, outperforming both U.S. stocks and gold over the same period
articles.body:
Bitcoin climbed to a one-month high near $74,000 on Friday, rallying roughly 5% in 24 hours after U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent signaled the Trump administration would take concrete steps to contain surging oil prices. The move extends a remarkable stretch of outperformance for the largest cryptocurrency, which has gained approximately 11% since the Iran conflict erupted on February 27 — a period in which both broad U.S. equity indices and gold have posted losses.

The rally appears driven by a convergence of macro relief and deeply negative market positioning. West Texas Intermediate crude fell to $94.50 per barrel on Friday from a near-$98 high a day earlier, easing fears about the stagflationary impact of sustained energy price spikes. Olu Sonola, head of U.S. economics at Fitch Ratings, warned that elevated oil prices could weaken consumer spending and constrain the Federal Reserve's ability to cut rates, noting that the broader growth outlook "increasingly looks fragile as downside risks accumulate."

Beneath the surface, bitcoin's futures market has been flashing signals that historically precede sharp reversals. According to K33 Research analyst Vetle Lunde, the 30-day average funding rate on perpetual futures has been negative for 14 consecutive days — the longest such streak since December 2022, when bitcoin was trading near $16,000 in the aftermath of the FTX collapse. Negative funding means short sellers are paying long holders to maintain their positions, an unusual dynamic that reflects extreme bearish sentiment. Open interest across perpetual and dated futures simultaneously jumped 9% in the past day to roughly 700,000 BTC, creating textbook conditions for a short squeeze.

Friday's advance would mark the first end-of-week gain since the Middle East conflict began, potentially signaling calmer weekend trading after weeks of Saturday and Sunday sell-offs. March is also shaping up as a potential inflection point: bitcoin is up about 8% month-to-date, and a positive close for the month would snap a five-month losing streak. The broader crypto market joined the rally, with Ethereum rising 7.6% to $2,200, Solana gaining 8.5%, and the CoinDesk 20 index climbing 5.4%.

Analysts are watching the $74,000 level closely. A decisive break above that threshold on high volume could open a path toward $80,000, while rejection risks trapping bitcoin in the consolidation range it has occupied since early February. For now, the combination of easing oil pressures, historically bearish positioning, and improving sentiment suggests the worst of the recent downturn may have passed — though the geopolitical backdrop remains deeply uncertain.

articles.headline: Strait of Hormuz Shipping Grinds to Halt as Insurers Balk
articles.subheadline: Port of Los Angeles chief says no amount of insurance money can entice carriers through the critical waterway amid Iran conflict
articles.body:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has effectively ceased as insurance markets refuse to underwrite vessels transiting the critical chokepoint, according to one of America's top port officials, underscoring the deepening economic fallout of the Iran conflict.

Gene Seroka, executive director of the Port of Los Angeles — the busiest container port in the Western Hemisphere — told Bloomberg on March 13 that shipping companies have neither the willingness nor the insurance coverage to send vessels through the strait. "The companies do not have an interest right now, nor is there enough money for insurance to transit those ships through the Strait," Seroka said, painting a stark picture of a global supply chain severed at one of its most vital arteries.

The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow passage between Iran and Oman, normally handles roughly 20 percent of the world's daily oil supply and serves as the sole maritime route out of the Persian Gulf. Any sustained disruption carries enormous consequences for global energy markets, consumer prices, and the economies of oil-exporting nations including Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The insurance industry's withdrawal from covering Hormuz transits mirrors the pattern seen in the Red Sea following Houthi attacks on commercial vessels, which forced massive rerouting of global trade around the Cape of Good Hope beginning in late 2023.

The collapse of commercial transit through Hormuz threatens to compound supply chain pressures already straining the global economy. Rerouting oil tankers and cargo ships around the southern tip of Africa or through alternative pipelines with limited capacity would add weeks to delivery times and billions in additional costs. For the Port of Los Angeles, which handles a significant share of trans-Pacific trade, the disruptions could ripple outward as energy costs climb and shipping schedules are thrown into disarray.

Seroka's comments represent one of the most direct acknowledgments yet from a major logistics figure that the Iran war has crossed a threshold from geopolitical crisis to full-blown commercial shipping emergency. With insurers — the financial backbone of maritime trade — effectively declaring the strait unnavigable for commercial purposes, pressure will mount on governments and international bodies to address both the security situation and the economic consequences radiating far beyond the Persian Gulf.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Nears $75K Trigger Where $3B in Options Hedging Could Amplify Rally
articles.subheadline: Short gamma positions held by options market makers could accelerate price moves, but macro headwinds loom large
articles.body:
Bitcoin climbed past $72,000 on Thursday, breaching its closely watched 50-day moving average and putting it within striking distance of a $75,000 level where a massive concentration of options market activity could supercharge volatility in either direction.

The catalyst lies in the options market, where market makers — the firms that provide liquidity on trading desks — are sitting on approximately $3 billion in net short gamma positions concentrated at the $75,000 strike price, according to Markus Thielen, founder of 10x Research. Short gamma positions create a dynamic where dealers must buy as prices rise and sell as prices fall, effectively amplifying market moves. Should bitcoin approach $75,000, the hedging activity required to rebalance these positions could generate a self-reinforcing buying wave. "As Bitcoin moves higher toward this region, dealer hedging flows can begin to play a more important role in shaping price dynamics," Thielen wrote in a client note.

The leading cryptocurrency was trading near $72,457 as of Thursday afternoon, up roughly 3% on the day, after clearing the 50-day simple moving average around $72,100 during European trading hours. Technical analysts point to $74,564 — the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of a January-through-February selloff — as the next significant resistance level. A failure to hold above the 50-day average, however, could invite renewed selling pressure back toward $70,000.

Not everyone is convinced the rally has legs. Alex Kuptsikevich, chief market analyst at FxPro, warned that deteriorating conditions across traditional markets could cap bitcoin's advance. The MOVE index, which tracks expected volatility in U.S. Treasury notes, surged more than 21% to 95 points on Thursday — its largest single-day jump since October 2024. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite fell 1.52% and 1.78% respectively, with both benchmarks sliding toward their 200-day lows. A strengthening U.S. dollar, rising oil prices amid the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz added to the risk-off mood. "We doubt Bitcoin will have the strength to withstand the wind for long," Kuptsikevich said.

Despite the macro turbulence, spot bitcoin ETFs recorded $53.8 million in net inflows on the day, bringing cumulative flows to $55.93 billion with total holdings of approximately 1.29 million BTC. Bitcoin's dominance of the broader crypto market stood at 59.44%, while its network hashrate held near record levels at 984 exahashes per second. Whether the cryptocurrency can punch through the $75,000 gamma trigger or buckle under broader market stress may hinge on a slate of U.S. economic data due Friday, including GDP revisions, core PCE inflation figures, and the preliminary University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading.

articles.headline: OPEC Oil Output Climbed in February as Saudi Arabia Ramped Up Production
articles.subheadline: The cartel's increased crude supply comes amid escalating regional tensions surrounding the conflict with Iran
articles.body:
OPEC's collective crude oil production rose last month, driven primarily by Saudi Arabia increasing its output as the Middle East confronts deepening instability tied to the war involving Iran, according to a Bloomberg survey published Thursday.

The production boost by the cartel's de facto leader signals a strategic calculation by Riyadh to maintain market stability — and its own revenues — at a moment when geopolitical risk might otherwise constrain supply. Saudi Arabia has historically used its significant spare capacity as a lever to manage global oil markets, and the latest move suggests the kingdom is prioritizing supply continuity even as conflict intensifies in its neighborhood.

The increase comes at a delicate juncture for energy markets. The ongoing military conflict involving Iran has raised persistent concerns about potential disruptions to crude flows through critical chokepoints such as the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil supply transits. By stepping up production, Saudi Arabia may be seeking to offset any supply losses that could emerge if the conflict escalates further, while simultaneously reassuring consuming nations that OPEC remains a reliable supplier.

Oil prices have been volatile in recent months as traders weigh the competing forces of war-driven supply risk against signs of softer global demand. The additional Saudi barrels could ease some of the geopolitical risk premium that has been embedded in crude benchmarks, though analysts caution that any direct disruption to Iranian or Gulf state exports could quickly overwhelm the incremental supply gains.

The production increase also raises questions about OPEC's internal cohesion. The group has spent years managing carefully calibrated output cuts to prop up prices, and any unilateral moves by member states risk straining those agreements. How the cartel navigates the tension between market management and wartime realities will be a defining test of its relevance in an increasingly fractured geopolitical landscape.

articles.headline: Rox AI Reaches $1.2 Billion Valuation With New Funding Round
articles.subheadline: The sales automation startup, backed by General Catalyst and Sequoia, deploys AI agents to overhaul CRM workflows
articles.body:
Rox AI, a startup building autonomous artificial intelligence agents for sales teams, has reached a $1.2 billion valuation following a new funding round led by returning investor General Catalyst, according to multiple people familiar with the matter.

The round, which closed in 2025, valued the company at unicorn status just a year after its founding. At the time the deal was struck, Rox was on track to close 2025 with $8 million in annual recurring revenue, two sources said. The company had previously disclosed $50 million in total funding across a Sequoia-led seed round and a General Catalyst-led Series A, with additional participation from GV. Neither Rox nor General Catalyst commented on the new valuation.

Founded in 2024 by Ishan Mukherjee — former chief growth officer at New Relic and co-founder of Pixie, the software monitoring firm New Relic acquired in 2020 — Rox positions itself as an intelligent revenue operating system. The platform integrates with existing enterprise tools such as Salesforce and Zendesk, deploying swarms of AI agents that monitor customer accounts, research sales prospects, and automatically update CRM records. The pitch is consolidation: rather than juggling a fragmented stack of sales software, companies can hand routine intelligence-gathering and data hygiene tasks to Rox's agents. Customers listed on the company's website include Ramp, MongoDB, and New Relic.

The valuation underscores persistent investor appetite for AI-driven sales tools despite an increasingly crowded landscape. Rox competes with established revenue intelligence players like Gong and Clari, AI sales development platforms such as 11x and Artisan, and a growing cohort of AI-native CRM startups. Among the newest entrants is Monaco, founded by former Brex president Sam Blond, which emerged from stealth in February. GV investor Dave Munichiello described Rox's approach as one that "levels up the CRM experience" by having agents "work constantly behind the scenes to monitor customer activity, identify potential risks and opportunities, and even suggest the best course of action."

With $8 million in ARR against a $1.2 billion valuation, the roughly 150-times revenue multiple reflects the premium investors are assigning to early-stage AI companies demonstrating rapid enterprise adoption. Whether Rox can grow into that valuation will depend on how quickly it can expand its customer base and fend off rivals in one of enterprise software's most competitive arenas.

articles.headline: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Halt as Iran Tightens Blockade
articles.subheadline: No confirmed commercial vessel transits recorded in either direction over 24-hour period, tracking data shows
articles.body:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has effectively ceased, with vessel-tracking data showing zero confirmed transits in either direction over the past 24 hours as Tehran intensifies its blockade of the world's most critical oil chokepoint.

The standstill, reported by Bloomberg on Thursday, marks a dramatic escalation in what has become the most significant disruption to global energy supply routes in decades. The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman, typically handles roughly 20 percent of the world's daily oil consumption — approximately 21 million barrels per day — along with substantial volumes of liquefied natural gas. A complete halt to traffic represents an unprecedented bottleneck for global energy markets and international trade.

Iran's hardening of the blockade raises urgent questions about the duration of the disruption and the diplomatic pathways available to restore passage. The strait, at its narrowest point just 21 miles wide, has long been considered one of the most strategically vulnerable pressure points in the global economy. Previous Iranian threats to close the waterway — during periods of heightened tensions over its nuclear program and in response to international sanctions — had never materialized to this extent. The current standstill suggests Tehran has moved beyond rhetoric to active enforcement.

The implications for global energy prices are severe. Even short-term disruptions to Hormuz traffic have historically triggered sharp spikes in crude oil and natural gas prices, and a sustained blockade would likely force importing nations in Asia and Europe to draw down strategic reserves while scrambling for alternative supply routes. Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates all depend heavily on the strait for their energy exports.

International maritime and defense officials are closely monitoring the situation, though details on potential military or diplomatic responses remain sparse. Shipping companies and insurers had already been rerouting vessels and reassessing risk premiums in recent weeks as tensions escalated.
